# A01P Altium Tutorial (S26)

## ESE 5160: IoT Edge Computing Deadline: Feb 2, 202611:59 PM EST

An **individual** submission to upload as a PDF to Gradescope.

No GitHub Classroom submission is required \- only a PDF on Gradescope. Work in a document (Word, Google Doc) to record the submission deliverables, then convert to PDF for submission.

**Late-day requests:** [https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8](https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8)   
Course Webpage: [https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/](https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/)	  
Remember to read the [ESE5160 S26 Assignment README](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DQJxfhTMBN1aE2uasZiKpqR0JOT77pXvb6tUPF7iKF8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.c5nyj2pvdk6p) before starting\!

**All the required questions are highlighted in green.**  
**Important notes are highlighted in yellow.**

# Learning Outcomes

* Get familiar with the Altium workspace on your machine or the lab computers  
* Learn how to go from component datasheets to fully digital representations of these parts, with component schematic symbols and PCB footprints as part of a component library  
* Draw schematics, leveraging our components from Altium’s trove of libraries  
* Lay out a rudimentary PCBA as part of a complete circuit board design  
* Understand how we’ll use Altium A365 & the UPenn ESE Workspace

# Errata

This section includes commonly asked questions about the assignment. It will be updated continuously throughout the assignment’s lifecycle, so please check it regularly.

(Currently nothing)

# 0\. Altium Licenses & Installation

The following is required to get access to an Altium license & Altium 365 \- and to complete this course\!

1. Follow the instructions on this webpage to gain access to Altium licenses: [Altium – Peter Detkin Lab](https://detkin.ese.upenn.edu/altium/)  
2. Once you have completed the registration above, post your registered SEAS email address on this ED post: [https://edstem.org/us/courses/91490/discussion/7529647](https://edstem.org/us/courses/91490/discussion/7529647)   
3. The Teaching Team will manually add you to the UPenn ESE Workspace

You may use Altium within the Detkin & Ketterer labs or on your own Windows laptop. If you’d like to run Altium on your personal computer, [download](https://www.altium.com/products/downloads) and install the latest version of Altium. If you’d like to run Altium on a Mac, one option is to purchase Parallels. You may have issues with the M1 or M2 Macs. If you’d like to run Altium on Linux, you’re on your own. 🙂

# 1\. Create Your First Project

Now you have your Altium designer installed. The first thing you need to do after logging in is to **open License Management**.  
![][image1]

Choose any **available Altium Designer** license.  
![][image2]

After you choose an active license, go to the left side of your application and create a project under UPenn ESE’s cloud (Altium 365 or A365).

![][image3]

For this assignment, you should create your project as **\[pennkey\]\_A01P** and save it in the University of Pennsylvania (ESE) Altium 365 Cloud: **Projects \> 2026S \> ESE 5160 Spring 2026 \> A01P**

* Example: my pennkey is nmcgill, so I would save the project as **nmcgill\_A01P**

![][image4]  
**Submission:** Nothing to be submitted.

# 2\. Altium Quick-Start Tutorial

In ESE5160, we will learn best practices for PCBA design. We believe that learning Altium is better achieved first by individual work to learn the basics, followed by one-on-one and group instruction led by the teaching team as the semester progresses.

Watch the Altium Designer tutorial and follow along. You’ll create an Altium project, capture a schematic, route a PCB, and generate output files: [Altium Designer Quick-Start Tutorial with Phil Salmony from Phil's Lab](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YTGzncKU5RY)

You can see the [Altium Academy YouTube channel](https://www.youtube.com/@AltiumAcademy) for other videos if you want some extra help.

**Submission:**

1.  Save your completed project as **\[pennkey\]\_A01P** in the University of Pennsylvania (ESE) Altium 365 vault, folder: **Projects \> 2026S \> ESE 5160 Spring 2026 \> A01P.**  Share the project with the **ESE5160\_S26\_TAs** group in Altium Vault.   
2. In your document, [submit a share link](#sharing-with-ta-group-on-a365) to this project (check to make sure the link works \- you should be able to view it in the web browser).   
3. In your document, include screenshots of the **completed schematic, completed PCB layout, and the 3D render/view of the PCB**

**NOTE:** As you complete this tutorial, please save and commit your project to the Altium cloud frequently. It’s good practice and may save your project one day\!

# 3\. Component Creation Tutorial

The tutorial in Part 2 is excellent, but it doesn’t cover how to create a component from scratch, including its schematic symbol and PCB footprint. If your component isn’t available in the UPenn ESE Vault or the Manufacturer Part Search, you’ll need to create it yourself. Here’s how:

## Step 1: Create Your Own Component Folder in Altium 365

1\. In the bottom-right corner of Altium Designer, click **Panels \> Explorer** to open the Explorer panel.

2\. Navigate to **Projects \> 2026S \> ESE 5160 Spring 2026 \> A01P \> A01P Components**. Right-click on the **A01P Components** folder and select **Add Subfolder \> Components**.  
![][image5]

3\. Name your component folder as **\[pennkey\]\_A01P\_Component**, and set folder access permissions:   
	Click **Share \> Add Role** and add **ESE5160\_S2S\_TAs**.  
Click **Add User** and add **Nick McGill**.  
**Important:** **DO NOT** grant access to anyone else to avoid polluting the public component folder. If you and your teammate need a shared customized component folder later, be mindful of folder accessibility.

## 

## Step 2: Create a Component

1\. Once your folder is created, click on it and select **Create Component**.  
![][image6]

2\. Name your component appropriately. For this homework, name it **RP2040**. A “Use Component Data” window may appear, where Altium attempts to match your component name with existing settings. For this assignment, you can click either **OK** or **Cancel**.

| ![][image7] | ![][image8] |
| :---- | :---- |

3\. Save your changes to the server by going to the Project Panel and selecting **Save to Server**. If the component appears as “New Component” in the panel, don’t worry—just save it to the server.  
![][image9]

## Step 3: Add Basic Symbol and Footprint

1\. In your component overview page, click on **Add Symbol Wizard** and **Add Footprint Wizard**. Use the default settings in the wizards (click **OK**, **Next**, and **Finish**). This will create a raw component with default configurations.  
![][image10]

2\. To **switch between** the symbol file, footprint file, and component overview, use the **Project Panel** or the **tabs at the top of Altium Designer’s UI**.  
![][image11]

## Step 4: Finalize the Component Setup

1\. In the component overview page, click **Select Target Folder** for both the symbol and footprint, and choose the component folder you created earlier.   
![][image12]

2\. Save your component to the server again via the Project Panel.  
![][image13]

## Step 5: Complete the Tutorial Video

1\. Follow the video tutorial linked below to create a symbol and footprint for **RP2040**:[Symbol and Footprint Creation | Altium Designer - Phil's Lab \#31](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DVErHmX-qyw)  
2\. Save your component to the server again via the Project Panel.  
![][image13]

## Tips

1\. Troubleshooting Save Errors  
If you encounter an error while saving your project to the server, double-check that you’ve completed **Step 4** to assign the correct target folder for your symbol and footprint.  
![][image14]

2\. You may have noticed that the method for creating components in the tutorial video differs from the one in the document. The video demonstrates how to create a local component library. While you can upload locally created components to our Altium 365 cloud for team collaboration and version control, directly creating a component folder in Altium 365 has its advantages:

* **Ease of Reuse**: Components created in the cloud folder can be easily reused across projects and teams.  
* **Standardization**: Altium 365 enforces a level of standardization since everyone accesses the same centralized library.

Additionally, you can control who has access to your component library by adjusting the folder's access permissions, ensuring only authorized users can utilize it.

**Submission:** 

1. Save your completed component in **\[pennkey\]\_A01P\_Component** Folder in the University of Pennsylvania (ESE) Altium 365 vault, folder: **Projects \> 2026S \> ESE 5160 Spring 2026 \> A01P \> A01P Components.**  Share the project with the **ESE5160\_S26\_TAs** group in Altium Vault.   
   1. Example naming with pennkey: **nmcgill\_A01P\_Component**  
2. In your document, provide the following artifacts:  
1. Screenshot of the RP2040 symbol  
2. Screenshot of the RP2040 footprint  
3. Screenshot of the RP2040 with imported 3D model  
4. [A shared link](#sharing-with-ta-group-on-a365) to your **\[pennkey\]\_A01P\_Component** folder (check to make sure the link works \- you should be able to view it in the web browser). 

# Uploading to UPenn’s Workspace in A365 Cloud

Below is how you can upload your project to UPenn's Workspace if you don't create it in the University of Pennsylvania (ESE) Altium 365 Cloud at first.

1\. **Right-click** your project and select **Make Project Available Online**.

![][image15]

2\. Rename your project as **\[pennkey\]\_A01P**

3\. Click **Advanced**, select the path as shown on the right side, **Projects \> 2026S \> ESE 5160 Spring 2026 \> A01P.** The image below is an example and doesn’t show the exact path you’ll need to choose.![][image16]

# Sharing with TA Group on A365 {#sharing-with-ta-group-on-a365}

![][image17]  
![][image18]

## Submission

Convert your document to a PDF and submit it on Gradescope.

| Note: Altium 365 does not display the component in your folder\! You can only see your components from Altium Desktop. |
| :---- |

# 

# Rubric

See the Gradescope assignment for the rubric details. For all questions, 0 points may be awarded if the submission is non-existent, very poorly done, or doesn’t compile (for firmware assignments).

This is a pass/fail assignment.
